quizQuestion:
Je doute qu'il {___}.
(I doubt he will come.)
Context: He hasn't come yet — the coming is still in the future.
sentenceTranslation: I doubt he will come.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif présent (futur/en cours)
explanation: "vienne" (subjonctif présent) is correct because the doubted action is still pending/future. Subjonctif présent follows a present-tense main clause when the subordinate action is simultaneous or future.

quizQuestion:
Je doute qu'il {___}.
(I doubt he has come / came.)
Context: The event (his arrival) is already in the past — you're doubting whether it happened.
sentenceTranslation: I doubt that he came.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif passé (accompli)
explanation: "soit venu" (subjonctif passé) is required because the doubted action is already completed. Subjonctif passé = que + auxiliaire au subjonctif présent + participe passé.

quizQuestion:
Il est important que … (choose the correct concordance form)
Context: You want him to come tomorrow — the action is future and not yet done.
sentenceTranslation: It is important that he come tomorrow.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif présent after impersonal expression
explanation: After impersonal expressions like "il est important que", use subjonctif présent when the desired action is future or ongoing. "qu'il vienne demain" is the correct form.

quizQuestion:
Je suis surpris(e) que … (choose the correct concordance form)
Context: It is now past 9 a.m. and you are surprised he already arrived.
sentenceTranslation: I am astonished that he arrived before 9 a.m.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif passé after emotion verb
explanation: The arrival is a completed past event. After emotion verbs (être surpris, être content, regretter), use subjonctif passé when the subordinate action happened before the main clause moment.

quizQuestion:
Je veux qu'elle {___} la vérité.
(I want her to tell the truth.)
Context: The telling is desired but hasn't happened yet.
sentenceTranslation: I want her to tell the truth.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif présent after vouloir que
explanation: "dise" (subjonctif présent) follows "vouloir que" when the desired action is future or anticipated. Never use subjonctif passé here — the action hasn't happened yet.

quizQuestion:
Je regrette qu'il {___} sans me dire au revoir.
(I regret that he left without saying goodbye.)
Context: He has already left — the departure is finished.
sentenceTranslation: I regret that he left without saying goodbye.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif passé after regretter que
explanation: "soit parti" (subjonctif passé) is required: the departure is a completed past action. "regretter que" + subj passé when the regretted event preceded the moment of regret.

quizQuestion:
Il faut … (choose the correct concordance form)
Context: You are telling someone she must leave right now — the leaving is imminent, not yet done.
sentenceTranslation: She must leave now.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif présent (simultané/immédiat)
explanation: When the subordinate action is simultaneous with or immediately following the main clause, use subjonctif présent. "qu'elle parte maintenant" is correct — she hasn't left yet.

quizQuestion:
Je suis étonné(e) … (choose the correct concordance form)
Context: You arrived and discovered she had already left before you.
sentenceTranslation: I am astonished that she left before me.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif passé (antériorité)
explanation: The departure happened before the moment of astonishment — it is an anterior completed action. "qu'elle soit partie avant moi" uses subjonctif passé (être verb: soit + partie with feminine agreement).

quizQuestion:
Sa mère exige … (choose the correct concordance form)
Context: The homework is not done — the mother demands it be done.
sentenceTranslation: His mother demands that he do his homework.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif présent after exiger que
explanation: The demanded action (doing homework) is pending/ongoing. "qu'il fasse ses devoirs" uses subjonctif présent of faire. Exiger que always takes the subjunctive for desired future action.

quizQuestion:
Sa mère est contente … (choose the correct concordance form)
Context: The mother is happy now because he already finished his homework earlier.
sentenceTranslation: His mother is happy that he has done his homework.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif passé after être content(e) que
explanation: The homework was completed before the mother's happiness — a completed prior action. "qu'il ait fait ses devoirs" uses subjonctif passé of faire (avoir verb: ait + fait).

quizQuestion:
Il est dommage qu'elle ne {___} pas à la fête.
(It's a shame she won't come to the party.)
Context: The party is tonight — she hasn't come yet.
sentenceTranslation: It's a shame she isn't coming to the party.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif présent after il est dommage que
explanation: "vienne" (subjonctif présent de venir) is correct. The regretted action is future/anticipated. Subjonctif présent follows impersonal expressions when the subordinate action is simultaneous or future.

quizQuestion:
Il est dommage qu'elle {___} sans nous prévenir.
(It's a shame she left without warning us.)
Context: She has already left.
sentenceTranslation: It's a shame she left without warning us.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif passé after il est dommage que (antériorité)
explanation: "soit partie" (subjonctif passé de partir) expresses a completed anterior action. The departure happened before the moment of regret. Partir takes être as auxiliary, so agreement applies: partie (f.).

quizQuestion:
Bien qu'il {___} fatigué, il continue à travailler.
(Although he is tired, he keeps working.)
Context: He is tired right now, at this moment.
sentenceTranslation: Although he is tired, he keeps working.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif présent after bien que (simultané)
explanation: "soit" (subjonctif présent d'être) is correct. The tiredness is simultaneous with the working. "Bien que" always triggers the subjunctive; the present form covers simultaneous or ongoing states.

quizQuestion:
Bien qu'il {___} toute la nuit, il ne comprend toujours pas.
(Although he studied all night, he still doesn't understand.)
Context: The studying is finished — it happened last night.
sentenceTranslation: Although he studied all night, he still doesn't understand.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif passé after bien que (antériorité)
explanation: "ait étudié" (subjonctif passé d'étudier) expresses a completed anterior action. When the bien que clause describes something that finished before the main clause, use subjonctif passé.

quizQuestion:
In modern spoken French, which subjunctive tense replaces the subjonctif imparfait after a past-tense main verb?
(e.g., Il voulait que tu… — what follows in modern usage?)
sentenceTranslation: He wanted you to come. → Il voulait que tu viennes. (modern rule)
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — modern rule: subj présent replaces subj imparfait
explanation: In modern French (spoken and written), the subjonctif imparfait (e.g., vinsses, fût) has been replaced by subjonctif présent even after a past-tense main verb. Only literary texts still use subj imparfait.

quizQuestion:
Identify the literary subjonctif imparfait form of être (3rd person singular) found in 19th-century French texts.
Modern equivalent: soit
sentenceTranslation: ...that it were / that it might be (literary)
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — fût (être, 3sg) — recognition
explanation: "fût" is the subjonctif imparfait of être (3rd person singular). Formed from passé simple stem fus- + -t (with circumflex accent). Modern French replaces this with "soit" (subj présent).
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Identify the literary subjonctif imparfait form of avoir (3rd person singular) found in 19th-century French texts.
Modern equivalent: ait
sentenceTranslation: ...that he/she might have (literary)
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — eût (avoir, 3sg) — recognition
explanation: "eût" is the subjonctif imparfait of avoir (3rd person singular). Stem eu- from passé simple + -t (with circumflex accent). In modern French this is replaced by "ait" (subj présent).
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Identify the literary subjonctif imparfait form of être (3rd person plural) found in 19th-century French texts.
Modern equivalent: soient
sentenceTranslation: ...that they might be (literary)
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — fussent (être, 3pl) — recognition
explanation: "fussent" is the subjonctif imparfait of être (3rd person plural). Stem fuss- + -ent. Modern spoken French uses "soient" (subj présent) in all registers except formal literary writing.
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Identify the literary subjonctif imparfait form of avoir (3rd person plural) found in 19th-century French texts.
Modern equivalent: aient
sentenceTranslation: ...that they might have (literary)
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — eussent (avoir, 3pl) — recognition
explanation: "eussent" is the subjonctif imparfait of avoir (3rd person plural). Stem euss- + -ent. In modern French, "aient" (subj présent) replaces this in all non-literary contexts.
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Identify the literary subjonctif imparfait form of faire (3rd person singular) found in classical French prose.
Modern equivalent: fasse
sentenceTranslation: ...that he/she might do/make (literary)
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — fît (faire, 3sg) — recognition
explanation: "fît" is the subjonctif imparfait of faire (3rd person singular). Passé simple stem fi- + -t (with circumflex accent). Modern French replaces this with "fasse" (subj présent).
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Identify the literary subjonctif imparfait form of venir (3rd person singular) found in 19th-century French texts.
Modern equivalent: vienne
sentenceTranslation: ...that he/she might come (literary)
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — vînt (venir, 3sg) — recognition
explanation: "vînt" is the subjonctif imparfait of venir (3rd person singular). Passé simple stem vin- + -t (with circumflex accent). Modern French uses "vienne" (subj présent) outside literary registers.
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Identify the literary subjonctif imparfait form of pouvoir (3rd person singular) found in classical French prose.
Modern equivalent: puisse
sentenceTranslation: ...that he/she might be able to (literary)
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — pût (pouvoir, 3sg) — recognition
explanation: "pût" is the subjonctif imparfait of pouvoir (3rd person singular). Passé simple stem pu- + -t (with circumflex accent). In modern spoken French, "puisse" (subj présent) is always used instead.
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Identify the literary subjonctif imparfait form of vouloir (3rd person singular) found in 19th-century French texts.
Modern equivalent: veuille
sentenceTranslation: ...that he/she might want / be willing to (literary)
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — voulût (vouloir, 3sg) — recognition
explanation: "voulût" is the subjonctif imparfait of vouloir (3rd person singular). Passé simple stem voul- + -ût. Modern French uses "veuille" (subj présent) in all everyday registers.
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Read this 19th-century sentence and identify the literary subjunctive form:
"Il cherchait un endroit où il {___} seul."
(He was looking for a place where he might be alone.)
sentenceTranslation: He was looking for a place where he might be alone.
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — fût in literary context (recognition)
explanation: "fût" (subj imparfait d'être, 3sg) is the literary form. The past-tense main verb "cherchait" triggered a past subjunctive under classical concordance rules. Modern French uses "soit" (subj présent) instead.
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Read this literary sentence and identify the subjonctif imparfait form:
"Elle doutait qu'il {___} la force de continuer."
(She doubted that he might have the strength to go on.)
sentenceTranslation: She doubted that he might have the strength to go on.
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — eût in literary context (recognition)
explanation: "eût" (subj imparfait d'avoir, 3sg) is the literary form triggered by the past-tense "doutait". In modern French, the rule is to use subjonctif présent ("ait") even after past-tense verbs.
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card

quizQuestion:
Il craignait que tu {___} en retard.
(He feared you would be late.)
Context: Modern spoken French rule — which subjunctive form follows "il craignait que"?
sentenceTranslation: He feared you would be late.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — modern rule: subj présent after past verb (spoken French)
explanation: In modern spoken French, "sois" (subjonctif présent) is used even after a past-tense trigger like "craignait". The old literary rule required subj imparfait ("fusses"), but this is archaic in everyday use.

quizQuestion:
Je suis content(e) qu'elle {___} le prix.
(I am happy that she won the prize.)
Context: The prize was awarded yesterday — the winning is a completed past fact.
sentenceTranslation: I am happy that she won the prize.
grammarPointLabel: Concordance des temps — subjonctif passé after être content(e) que (antériorité)
explanation: "ait gagné" (subjonctif passé de gagner) is required because the winning happened before the moment of happiness. Subjonctif passé = ait + participe passé (avoir verb, no agreement needed here).

quizQuestion: In the Tatoeba sentence "Bien qu'il plût, je devais sortir", what tense is "plût" and in which register is it used?
sentenceTranslation: Although it was raining, I had to go out.
grammarPointLabel: Subjonctif imparfait — plût (pleuvoir) — literary register identification
explanation: "plût" is the subjonctif imparfait of pleuvoir (to rain). The circumflex distinguishes it from "plut" (passé simple indicatif). This form appears only in literary/formal written French; modern speech uses "pleuve" (subj présent).
visualDescription: French grammar literary form recognition card
